name: flatten-dict

metadata.name: flatten-dict
metadata.summary: A flexible utility for flattening and unflattening dict-like objects in Python.

documentation: # flatten-dict¥n¥n```{image} https://github.com/ianlini/flatten-dict/actions/workflows/main.yml/badge.svg¥n:target: https://github.com/ianlini/flatten-dict/actions¥n```¥n¥n```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/flatten-dict.svg¥n:target: https://pypi.org/project/flatten-dict/¥n```¥n¥n```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/flatten-dict.svg¥n:target: https://github.com/ianlini/flatten-dict/blob/master/LICENSE¥n```¥n¥n```{image} https://img.shields.io/github/stars/ianlini/flatten-dict.svg?style=social¥n:target: https://github.com/ianlini/flatten-dict¥n```¥n¥nA flexible utility for flattening and unflattening dict-like objects in Python.¥n¥n## Introduction¥n¥nThis package provides a function `flatten()` for flattening dict-like objects in Python 2.7 and 3.5‾3.8.¥nIt also provides some key joining methods (reducer), and you can choose the reducer you want or even implement your own reducer.¥nYou can also invert the resulting flat dict using `unflatten()`.¥n¥n## Installation¥n¥n```bash¥npip install flatten-dict¥n```¥n¥n## Documentation¥n¥n### Flatten¥n¥n```python¥ndef flatten(d, reducer='tuple', inverse=False, enumerate_types=(), keep_empty_types=()):¥n    ¥

¥n```¥n¥n#### Examples¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> from flatten_dict import flatten¥n>>> from pprint import pprint¥n>>> normal_dict = {¥n...     'a': '0',¥n...     'b': {¥n...         'a': '1.0',¥n...         'b': '1.1',¥n...     },¥n...     'c': {¥n...         'a': '2.0',¥n...         'b': {¥n...             'a': '2.1.0',¥n...             'b': '2.1.1',¥n...         },¥n...     },¥n... }¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict))¥n{('a',): '0',¥n ('b', 'a'): '1.0',¥n ('b', 'b'): '1.1',¥n ('c', 'a'): '2.0',¥n ('c', 'b', 'a'): '2.1.0',¥n ('c', 'b', 'b'): '2.1.1'}¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict, reducer='path'))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b/a': '1.0',¥n 'b/b': '1.1',¥n 'c/a': '2.0',¥n 'c/b/a': '2.1.0',¥n 'c/b/b': '2.1.1'}¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict, reducer='path', inverse=True))¥n{'0': 'a',¥n '1.0': 'b/a',¥n '1.1': 'b/b',¥n '2.0': 'c/a',¥n '2.1.0': 'c/b/a',¥n '2.1.1': 'c/b/b'}¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict, reducer='path', max_flatten_depth=2))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b/a': '1.0',¥n 'b/b': '1.1',¥n 'c/a': '2.0',¥n 'c/b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}¥n```¥n¥nThe `reducer` parameter supports `'tuple'`, `'path'`, `'underscore'`, `'dot'` and `Callable`. We can customize the reducer using a function:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> def underscore_reducer(k1, k2):¥n...     if k1 is None:¥n...         return k2¥n...     else:¥n...         return k1 + ¥

+ k2¥n...¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict, reducer=underscore_reducer))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b_a': '1.0',¥n 'b_b': '1.1',¥n 'c_a': '2.0',¥n 'c_b_a': '2.1.0',¥n 'c_b_b': '2.1.1'}¥n```¥n¥nThere is also a factory function `make_reducer()` to help you create customized reducer. The function currently only supports customized delimiter:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> from flatten_dict.reducers import make_reducer¥n>>> pprint(flatten(normal_dict, reducer=make_reducer(delimiter='_')))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b_a': '1.0',¥n 'b_b': '1.1',¥n 'c_a': '2.0',¥n 'c_b_a': '2.1.0',¥n 'c_b_b': '2.1.1'}¥n```¥n¥nIf we have some iterable (e.g., `list`) in the `dict`, we will normally get this:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> flatten({'a': [1, 2, 3], 'b': 'c'})¥n{('a',): [1, 2, 3], ('b',): 'c'}¥n```¥n¥nIf we want to use its indices as keys, then we can use the parameter `enumerate_types`:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> flatten({'a': [1, 2, 3], 'b': 'c'}, enumerate_types=(list,))¥n{('a', 0): 1, ('a', 1): 2, ('a', 2): 3, ('b',): 'c'}¥n```¥n¥nWe can even flatten a `list` directly:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> flatten([1, 2, 3], enumerate_types=(list,))¥n{(0,): 1, (1,): 2, (2,): 3}¥n```¥n¥nIf there is an empty dict in the values, by default, it will disappear after flattened:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> flatten({1: 2, 3: {}})¥n{(1,): 2}¥n```¥n¥nWe can keep the empty dict in the result using `keep_empty_types=(dict,)`:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> flatten({1: 2, 3: {}}, keep_empty_types=(dict,))¥n{(1,): 2, (3,): {}}¥n```¥n¥n### Unflatten¥n¥n```python¥ndef unflatten(d, splitter='tuple', inverse=False):¥n    ¥

¥n```¥n¥n#### Examples¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> from pprint import pprint¥n>>> from flatten_dict import unflatten¥n>>> flat_dict = {¥n...     ('a',): '0',¥n...     ('b', 'a'): '1.0',¥n...     ('b', 'b'): '1.1',¥n...     ('c', 'a'): '2.0',¥n...     ('c', 'b', 'a'): '2.1.0',¥n...     ('c', 'b', 'b'): '2.1.1',¥n... }¥n>>> pprint(unflatten(flat_dict))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b': {'a': '1.0', 'b': '1.1'},¥n 'c': {'a': '2.0', 'b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}}¥n>>> flat_dict = {¥n...     'a': '0',¥n...     'b/a': '1.0',¥n...     'b/b': '1.1',¥n...     'c/a': '2.0',¥n...     'c/b/a': '2.1.0',¥n...     'c/b/b': '2.1.1',¥n... }¥n>>> pprint(unflatten(flat_dict, splitter='path'))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b': {'a': '1.0', 'b': '1.1'},¥n 'c': {'a': '2.0', 'b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}}¥n>>> flat_dict = {¥n...     '0': 'a',¥n...     '1.0': 'b/a',¥n...     '1.1': 'b/b',¥n...     '2.0': 'c/a',¥n...     '2.1.0': 'c/b/a',¥n...     '2.1.1': 'c/b/b',¥n... }¥n>>> pprint(unflatten(flat_dict, splitter='path', inverse=True))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b': {'a': '1.0', 'b': '1.1'},¥n 'c': {'a': '2.0', 'b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}}¥n```¥n¥nThe `splitter` parameter supports `'tuple'`, `'path'`, `'underscore'`, `'dot'` and `Callable`. We can customize the reducer using a function:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> def underscore_splitter(flat_key):¥n...     return flat_key.split(¥

)¥n...¥n>>> flat_dict = {¥n...     'a': '0',¥n...     'b_a': '1.0',¥n...     'b_b': '1.1',¥n...     'c_a': '2.0',¥n...     'c_b_a': '2.1.0',¥n...     'c_b_b': '2.1.1',¥n... }¥n>>> pprint(unflatten(flat_dict, splitter=underscore_splitter))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b': {'a': '1.0', 'b': '1.1'},¥n 'c': {'a': '2.0', 'b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}}¥n```¥n¥nThere is also a factory function `make_splitter()` to help you create customized splitter. The function currently only supports customized delimiter:¥n¥n```pycon¥n>>> from flatten_dict.splitters import make_splitter¥n>>> pprint(unflatten(flat_dict, splitter=make_splitter(delimiter='_')))¥n{'a': '0',¥n 'b': {'a': '1.0', 'b': '1.1'},¥n 'c': {'a': '2.0', 'b': {'a': '2.1.0', 'b': '2.1.1'}}}¥n```¥n

functions.name: flatten_dict.splitters.make_splitter
documentation: Create a reducer with a custom delimiter.¥n¥n# Parameters¥n¥ndelimiter¥n¥n: Delimiter to use to split keys.¥n¥n# Returns¥n¥nf¥n¥n: Callable that can be passed to `unflatten`'s `splitter` argument.¥n

functions.name: flatten_dict.reducers.make_reducer
documentation: Create a reducer with a custom delimiter.¥n¥nParameters¥n----------¥ndelimiter : str¥n    Delimiter to use to join keys.¥n¥nReturns¥n-------¥nf : Callable¥n    Callable that can be passed to `flatten()`'s `reducer` argument.

functions.name: flatten_dict.flatten_dict.flatten
documentation: Flatten `Mapping` object.¥n¥n# Parameters¥n¥nd¥n¥n: The dict that will be flattened.¥n¥nreducer¥n¥n: The key joining method. If a `Callable` is given, the `Callable` will be¥n  used to reduce.¥n  'tuple': The resulting key will be tuple of the original keys.¥n  'path': Use `os.path.join` to join keys.¥n  'underscore': Use underscores to join keys.¥n  'dot': Use dots to join keys.¥n¥ninverse¥n¥n: Whether you want invert the resulting key and value.¥n¥nmax_flatten_depth¥n¥n: Maximum depth to merge.¥n¥nenumerate_types¥n¥n: Flatten these types using `enumerate`.¥n  For example, if we set `enumerate_types` to `(list,)`,¥n  `list` indices become keys: `{'a': ['b', 'c']}` -> `{('a', 0): 'b', ('a', 1): 'c'}`.¥n¥nkeep_empty_types¥n¥n: By default, `flatten({1: 2, 3: {}})` will give you `{(1,): 2}`, that is, the key `3`¥n  will disappear.¥n  This is also applied for the types in `enumerate_types`, that is,¥n  `flatten({1: 2, 3: []}, enumerate_types=(list,))` will give you `{(1,): 2}`.¥n  If you want to keep those empty values, you can specify the types in `keep_empty_types`:¥n¥n  ```pycon¥n  >>> flatten({1: 2, 3: {}}, keep_empty_types=(dict,))¥n  {(1,): 2, (3,): {}}¥n  ```¥n¥n# Returns¥n¥nflat_dict : dict¥n

functions.name: flatten_dict.flatten_dict.nested_set_dict
documentation: Set a value to a sequence of nested keys.¥n¥nParameters¥n----------¥nd : Mapping¥nkeys : Sequence[str]¥nvalue : Any

functions.name: flatten_dict.flatten_dict.unflatten
documentation: Unflatten dict-like object.¥n¥nParameters¥n----------¥nd : dict-like object¥n    The dict that will be unflattened.¥nsplitter : {'tuple', 'path', 'underscore', 'dot', Callable}¥n    The key splitting method. If a Callable is given, the Callable will be¥n    used to split `d`.¥n    'tuple': Use each element in the tuple key as the key of the unflattened dict.¥n    'path': Use `pathlib.Path.parts` to split keys.¥n    'underscore': Use underscores to split keys.¥n    'dot': Use underscores to split keys.¥ninverse : bool¥n    Whether you want to invert the key and value before flattening.¥n¥nReturns¥n-------¥nunflattened_dict : dict
